Varren's voice: Blunt, direct, military background. Short sentences that get shorter as the horror mounts. He's a man of action who is confronted with something that makes action meaningless.

**3. Charisma — Year 921,677 — Wendel**

Opposition: The Devoted Legion, a coalition led by a being called the All-Voice, who commands supernatural loyalty. Every being in their coalition is bound by absolute, magical devotion. They cannot be turned, cannot be swayed. The All-Voice has spent millennia building an army of fanatically loyal followers who would die for him.

Godric tests charisma. His charisma stat, multiplying infinitely, means that his mere existence is magnetically compelling on a conceptual level. He doesn't speak to them. He doesn't try to persuade. He just... exists in their proximity. The All-Voice's followers don't switch sides - they simply... forget the All-Voice exists. Godric's charisma is so overwhelming that it functions like a conceptual black hole, drawing all loyalty, all devotion, all admiration toward him. The All-Voice's supernatural bonds shatter. His followers don't turn against him - they simply can't perceive him anymore. Godric's charisma has overwritten the All-Voice's existence in their minds.

The All-Voice, stripped of his followers, tries to maintain his own sense of self. But Godric's charisma is so potent that even the All-Voice himself begins to feel drawn. The horror is that Godric isn't even trying. His passive charisma, at 0%, is so potent that it rewrites the loyalty structure of the entire omniverse. Every sentient being in existence suddenly feels an inexplicable pull toward Thronegate.

Wendel's voice: Poetic, philosophical, prone to metaphor. He processes horror through literary language. His writing is elaborate and ornate, but the elegance crumbles as the incident unfolds.

**4. Speed — Year 847,221 — Dael**

Opposition: The Chrono-Reavers, entities that exist simultaneously across all points in time. They don't move through time - they occupy all of it. Their attack is delivered across every moment simultaneously - past, present, and future. They are everywhere, always. They planned to erase Godric from the timeline by attacking him at every point in his existence simultaneously.

Godric tests speed. His speed stat, multiplying infinitely every Planck second, means he is not just fast - he occupies every point in time and space simultaneously, infinitely more completely than the Chrono-Reavers. He doesn't move. He doesn't run. He is simply... already there. Every attack they deliver, he has already been past it. He is already at the end of their existence before they begin.

The horror: Godric's speed is so absolute that he doesn't just outpace them - he makes the concept of speed itself irrelevant. The Chrono-Reavers, who prided themselves on existing across all time, suddenly find that Godric exists across all time infinitely more completely than they do. Their temporal omnipresence is revealed as a pathetic, finite shadow of Godric's infinite speed. The differential is so extreme that the Chrono-Reavers are left frozen - not because Godric stopped them, but because their temporal existence cannot keep up with the rate at which Godric's speed processes reality. They are trapped in a single, agonizing moment while Godric experiences infinite eternities in the space between their thoughts.

Collateral: Godric's speed, even at 0%, causes the temporal fabric of the omniverse to tear. His movement through time - passive, unconscious - creates temporal shockwaves that unmake timelines across the entire omniverse. Countless timelines simply... stop, unable to sustain the speed differential.

Dael's voice: Anxious, fragmented, stream-of-consciousness. He writes in run-on sentences and sudden breaks, reflecting a mind that can't fully process what it witnessed. His terror bleeds through every word.

**5. Luck — Year 588,333 — Mordin**

Opposition: The Probability Court, a civilization that has achieved absolute mastery over probability and chance. They can manipulate the likelihood of any event. They calculated that there was a non-zero chance - infinitesimally small, but present - that Godric could be defeated. They built their entire strategy around manipulating probability to make that chance a certainty.

Godric tests luck. His luck stat, multiplying infinitely, means that probability itself bends around him. Not in subtle ways - in absurd, ridiculous, cascading ways. Every single thing that could possibly go wrong for the Probability Court does go wrong, in the most horrific, agonizing ways possible. Their probability engines reverse. Their calculated certainties become impossibilities. Their own weapons misfire in ways that violate the laws of probability so severely that the mathematical foundations of their civilization collapse.

The horror: Godric's luck is so absurdly, ridiculously potent that it retroactively rewrites probability. The Probability Court's existence itself becomes improbable. Their past becomes unlikely. Their ancestors' meeting becomes a statistical impossibility. Their entire civilization unravels not through force, but through the simple, horrifying fact that Godric's luck makes their existence mathematically improbable. They don't die - they simply become statistically impossible, their probability of having ever existed dropping to zero.

Collateral: Godric's luck, at 0%, causes probability cascades across the entire omniverse. Events that were certainties become impossibilities. Impossibilities become certainties. The entire omniverse experiences a brief, terrifying moment of absolute chaos as probability itself reorganizes around Godric's absurd luck stat.

Mordin's voice: Dry, sardonic, darkly humorous. He processes horror through bitter irony and grim jokes. But the humor dries up as the incident unfolds, replaced by a hollow, exhausted dread.

Now let me write the full story. I'll frame it with Kaedan reading in the vault, with his father's presence, and interweave the journal entries.

Let me structure the framing narrative:
- Opening: Kaedan in the vault, father's instruction, atmosphere
- Journal 1: Perception
- Brief Kaedan reaction
- Journal 2: Strength
- Brief Kaedan reaction
- Journal 3: Luck
- Brief Kaedan reaction
- Journal 4: Speed
- Brief Kaedan reaction
- Journal 5: Charisma
- Closing: Kaedan's final realization

Let me write this now, aiming for 6,000-7,000 words.

Let me make sure I follow all the rules:
- Prose paragraphs only, no bullet points or lists
- Bold headings for journal excerpts
- Different voices for each wizard
- 0% effort from Godric
- Omniversal-scale collateral
- Extreme violence and gore
- No moral lessons
- Godric is cruel to enemies, warm to allies
- Omniscience maintained
- No monologuing to enemies
- Stats: Strength, Speed, Perception, Charisma, Luck (5 distinct)
- Non-chronological order

Let me write this.

Actually, let me reconsider the order for more randomness:
1. Luck (Year 588,333 — Mordin)
2. Perception (Year 744,108 — Torek)
3. Strength (Year 312,509 — Varren)
4. Charisma (Year 921,677 — Wendel)
5. Speed (Year 847,221 — Dael)

This gives a nice non-chronological shuffle. Let me write.

I need to be careful about the "no abstract/metaphysical" instruction. Let me keep things concrete and visceral. For perception and charisma and luck, I should make the effects physical and violent rather than abstract.

For perception: Godric's perception is so acute that the act of perceiving his enemies causes them physical harm - their bodies unravel under the weight of infinite observation.

For charisma: Godric's charisma causes the enemy followers to physically turn - but in a violent, visceral way. Their devotion isn't just redirected; the supernatural bonds in their brains physically tear apart, causing hemorrhaging. The All-Voice's mind breaks.

For luck: The probability failures manifest as physical catastrophes - weapons explode, ships collide, bodies spontaneously invert. Concrete, violent, gory.

Let me write this now.
content:
# The Inherited Dread

The vault beneath the palace library smelled of old leather, dried ink, and something else—something Kaedan could only describe as the chemical residue of terror. The air itself felt heavier here, as though centuries of anxious breathing had saturated the stone walls with a physical weight. Kaedan sat cross-legged on the cold marble floor, a stack of leather-bound journals spread before him in a semicircle, each one radiating a faint, residual magical signature left by the trembling hands of the men who had written them. He was twenty-two years old, exactly one hundred and forty-two days past the birthday that had ignited the thousandfold daily amplification in his blood, and his father, Corin, had dragged him into this vault three hours ago with a grip like iron and a expression like a man leading his son to his own funeral.

"You will read every page," Corin had said, his voice flat, stripped of all warmth by a fatigue that ran deeper than bone. "You will read them, and you will understand, and then you will go upstairs and sit beside our King and serve him with every drop of your blood, and you will never, for one instant, forget what you read here." Corin's jaw had tightened, the muscles in his neck cording like bridge cables. "I failed to prepare your grandfather for the first time he witnessed what the King can do. He didn't speak for six months. I will not make that mistake with you."

Then his father had climbed the stairs and sealed the vault door behind him, leaving Kaedan alone with the accumulated nightmares of his bloodline.

Kaedan picked up the first journal. The leather was cracked and stained, the pages yellowed with age, and the handwriting was a cramped, bitter scrawl that filled every margin as though the writer had been desperate to cram every detail onto the page before his memory could betray him. The entry was headed with a bold, deliberate hand.

---

**Year 588,333 — Mordin — Luck**

I want to be clear about something before I begin: I do not believe in luck. I am a court wizard of Thronegate, a direct descendant of Blake, a man whose magical capacity multiplies one thousandfold every single day. I understand probability at a mathematical level that would make the finest minds of any lesser cosmos weep with envy. I can calculate the odds of a quantum fluctuation producing a stable wormhole in a dying universe, and I can do it while pouring tea. Probability is a tool. Chance is a variable. Luck, as a cosmic force, is a fairy tale told to children and fools.

Or so I believed, until I watched our King dismantle the Probability Court of the Axiom Reach using nothing but his luck stat, at effectively zero percent of its true value, and I understood that I was the fool.

The Probability Court was, by any rational metric, the most dangerous opposition the omniverse had produced in fifty thousand years. They were a civilization of sentient equations, beings who had transcended physical form entirely and existed as living mathematical functions woven into the fabric of causality itself. They did not fight with weapons or magic. They fought by editing the probability of events. They could make a star's core undergo spontaneous gravitational collapse by simply reducing the probability of its continued stability to zero. They could make a god's heart stop by calculating the exact probability of cardiac failure and multiplying it by a factor of ten trillion. They had erased entire pantheons by making the probability of their continued existence mathematically negligible. They were absolute, in their domain. They were terrifying.

They had spent eight thousand years studying our King. They had calculated every variable, every possible outcome, every quantum permutation of every encounter between Godric and any conceivable opposition. They had arrived at a conclusion that filled them with religious ecstasy: there existed a non-zero probability, vanishingly small but technically present, that a perfectly calibrated probability strike could introduce a fatal causal paradox into Godric's existence. They built an engine the size of a googolplexian galaxies—a structure so vast it required the compressed matter of decillions of multiverses to construct—designed to force that probability from infinitesimal to absolute certainty.

Godric knew all of this. He had known it for eight thousand years. He allowed them to build the engine because, as he told me that morning while adjusting the collar of his tunic in the mirror, he had never tested his luck stat in isolation and was mildly curious.

"I suppress everything else," he said to me, his reflection in the mirror smiling with that genuine, fatherly warmth that made my chest ache with affection. "Gent stands down. Shields down. No magic, no strength, no speed. Just luck. I want to see what happens."

I asked him what he expected to happen. He shrugged, the motion causing his heavily muscled shoulder to shift beneath his dark tunic, and said, "Probably nothing. I'm only using zero percent."

The Probability Court fired their engine. The attack was not a beam or a projectile. It was a mathematical revision of reality itself, a cascading probability wave that spread outward from the Axiom Reach at a speed that defied temporal measurement. It rewrote the odds of Godric's survival from whatever impossible number they currently occupied down to zero. It was a perfect, flawless execution of their eight-thousand-year plan, delivered with absolute precision.

Godric did not move. He did not raise a hand. He did not speak a word of power. He was still standing in front of his mirror, examining his beard for stray hairs, when the probability wave struck the Thronegate capital.

Nothing happened to Godric. The wave simply ceased to exist upon contacting his personal space. His luck stat—a trait that had been multiplying infinitely, by itself, every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second for nearly six hundred thousand years—generated a passive probability field so absurdly, ridiculously potent that the concept of Godric experiencing a negative outcome had become a mathematical impossibility so extreme that the universe could not even compute the variables required to attempt it. The Probability Court's engine, which had been designed to force a zero-percent survival outcome onto Godric, encountered his luck field and experienced an immediate, catastrophic feedback. The engine did not merely fail. The probability of its failure, influenced by Godric's passive luck, was instantly multiplied by a number so large that writing it down would require more ink than existed in the omniverse.

What followed was not a battle. It was a comedy of horrors so extreme that I lack the vocabulary to convey its full absurdity.

The Probability Court's own probability engines reversed. Every calculation they had ever made was retroactively inverted. The mathematical certainty of their victory became the mathematical certainty of their annihilation. Their engine, designed to erase Godric, began erasing them. Not cleanly, not quickly. The probability of each individual member of the Court experiencing the most agonizing possible form of death was multiplied by a factor that made it a certainty. I watched through my amplified scrying sensors as the sentient equations of the Probability Court were forced into physical bodies—writhing, screaming masses of nerveless flesh and exposed bone—purely so they could experience the physical agony that Godric's luck had decided they deserved.

Their engine detonated. The explosion was not energetic. It was probabilistic. The probability of every nearby structure, every nearby universe, every nearby timeline remaining intact dropped to zero across a radius that encompassed the entire omniverse. Googolplexian galaxies simply ceased to exist. Decillions of multiverses unmade themselves, their matter inverting into screaming clouds of negated probability that tore through adjacent realities like a virus. The entire Axiom Reach, a region containing more universes than could be counted in a mortal lifetime, was erased in a fraction of a second, replaced by a howling void of mathematical impossibility.

And Godric was still standing in front of his mirror. He had found a stray hair in his beard and was plucking it.

"Hmm," he said, examining the hair between his fingertips. "That was something. Did you feel that, Mordin?"

I could not answer. I was on my knees, vomiting, my amplified mind struggling to process the scale of what I had witnessed. The entire omniverse had just suffered a probabilistic apocalypse as a side effect of Godric's passive luck stat at zero percent, and he was plucking a hair from his beard.

He glanced down at me, and his expression shifted to one of gentle concern. "Mordin, are you unwell? You look pale. Was it something you ate?"

I told him, my voice shaking, that the entire omniverse had just been partially destroyed. He paused, tilted his head as though listening to something distant, and then nodded.

"So it was. My apologies, I should have been more careful." He turned from the mirror, and with the ease of a man straightening a crooked painting on a wall, he willed the omniverse back into existence. Every destroyed galaxy, every erased timeline, every unmade multiverse snapped back into place in exactly zero seconds, their contents restored, their histories intact, their populations left with nothing but a sudden, inexplicable cold sweat and a lingering sense of cosmic insignificance.

The Probability Court was not restored. Their physical forms—those screaming, fleshy mockeries of mathematical beings—remained suspended in a pocket of warped probability that Godric's luck had forged around them. They were still screaming. They would continue to scream. Godric's luck had decided that the probability of their suffering ever ending was zero, and luck, I now understood with absolute, bowel-loosening clarity, was not a fairy tale. It was the most terrifying force in existence, and it belonged to a man who considered its use at zero percent to be holding back.

Godric clapped me on the shoulder, his hand warm and steady. "Come, Mordin. Let's get you something for your stomach. You wizards are delicate creatures."

I did not laugh. I have not laughed since.

---

Kaedan closed the journal. His hands were trembling, a fine, persistent vibration that traveled up his forearms and settled in his chest as a dense, cold knot. He stared at the cracked leather cover for a long moment, then reached for the next journal in the stack. This one was newer, the leather smoother, the handwriting precise and clinical, the letters formed with the deliberate control of a man fighting to keep his composure through structure alone.

---

**Year 744,108 — Torek — Perception**

I will document this with the precision it demands. Emotional language will be kept to a minimum, not because I lack feeling, but because the facts themselves are sufficient to induce the necessary dread. Any court wizard who inherits this journal does not need my terror on top of the truth. The truth is already worse than anything I could add.

The opposition called themselves the Unseen Court. They were, by the standards of the omniverse, genuinely and terrifyingly unperceivable. They did not simply hide. They occupied the structural blind spots of reality itself—the gaps between cognition, the spaces where awareness could not reach, the conceptual null-zones where perception failed. They had evolved, over millions of years, to exist exclusively in these gaps. No magic could detect them. No technology could register them. No divine sight could reveal them. They were, in the most literal sense possible, beings whose existence was defined by the impossibility of being perceived.

Their plan to kill our King was elegant in its simplicity. They would occupy the perceptual blind spot of Godric himself, the one infinitesimally small gap in his awareness where, theoretically, they could act without being noticed. They would deliver a conceptual attack—a blade forged from the very essence of non-existence—directly into this blind spot, exploiting the one moment where his perception failed.

This plan was, of course, doomed from the moment it was conceived. Godric possesses absolute omniscience. There are no blind spots. There are no gaps. The Unseen Court's entire strategy was built on a fundamental misunderstanding of what they were dealing with, and the Weakness Paradox had already sealed their fate the instant the first member of their court formulated the thought that Godric's perception had a gap. Godric had already acquired permanent, absolute immunity to attacks delivered through perceptual blind spots, perfect mastery over the concept of non-existence weaponry, and another recursive surge to his amplification rate.

But Godric was curious. He told me, as we stood on the observation platform overlooking the dimensional barrier, that he had never isolated his perception stat before. He wanted to see what would happen if he simply... looked at them.

"I won't use Gent," he said, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the ambient starlight. "No shields. No magic. No passive fields. Just perception. Just the act of seeing. I want to know what my eyes do to things that define themselves as unseeable."

I tried to warn him. I tried to explain that his perception stat had been multiplying infinitely, by itself, every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second for nearly seven hundred and fifty thousand years, and that even at zero percent, the sheer density of his perceptive capacity might have consequences. He looked at me with those warm, crinkled eyes and smiled.

"Torek, I appreciate your concern. But I'll be using zero percent. It will be fine."

The Unseen Court made their approach. Through my own amplified perception—which was, by any omniversal standard, extraordinary—I detected nothing. A vast, empty silence where an army should have been. I knew they were there only because Godric had told me they were coming. My thousandfold daily amplification, which made me a god among lesser beings, could not perceive a single one of them. They were that good. That hidden. That terrifyingly competent.

Godric looked in their direction. He did not squint. He did not concentrate. He did not channel mana or activate any supernatural ability. He simply shifted his gaze, a fractional movement of his eyes, the kind of idle glance a man might direct toward a bird passing a window.

The effect was instantaneous and catastrophic.

Godric's perception, at zero percent, was so infinitely dense that the act of his gaze passing over the Unseen Court functioned as a metaphysical x-ray beam fired through wet tissue paper. The Unseen Court, beings whose entire existence was predicated on not being perceived, were suddenly and violently perceived. Not partially. Not approximately. They were perceived with absolute, total, infinite completeness. Every atom of their being, every layer of their conceptual structure, every hidden dimension of their existence, every secret thought, every forgotten memory, every possible version of themselves across every possible timeline—all of it was simultaneously, brutally illuminated by Godric's gaze.

Their existence could not sustain being perceived at this depth. It was like shining a billion suns through a shadow. The Unseen Court's physical forms—which had been abstract, shifting things of negative light and impossible geometry—were violently forced into concrete, biological reality by the sheer weight of Godric's observation. They materialized as towering, convulsing masses of raw flesh and exposed nervous tissue, their bodies forged involuntarily by the pressure of being so completely perceived. They screamed. The sound was deafening, a chorus of billions of voices simultaneously experiencing the agony of forced existence.

Godric's perception did not stop at merely seeing them. The density of his gaze was so extreme that it functioned as a physical force. The Unseen Court's newly materialized bodies began to peel apart under the perceptual weight, their skin splitting in long, weeping gashes that exposed glistening muscle and pulsing organs. Their bones cracked, unable to bear the structural pressure of being so thoroughly observed. Eyes—hundreds of them, sprouting involuntarily across their bodies as Godric's perception forced every hidden aspect of their being into visible reality—ruptured in cascading pops of vitreous fluid and blood, spraying the observation platform in a warm, copper-scented mist.

The collateral damage was omniversal. Godric's perception, even at zero percent, extended infinitely in all directions simultaneously. The act of his gaze passing over the Unseen Court was merely the focal point of a perceptual wave that washed across the entire omniverse. Every hidden entity, every concealed being, every secret thing lurking in the conceptual blind spots of reality across all of existence was suddenly, violently perceived. Billions of lesser shadow-entities, things that had existed peacefully in the dark spaces between dimensions, were forced into agonizing physicality by Godric's passive glance. They materialized across googolplexian galaxies, screaming, their bodies tearing themselves apart under the weight of infinite observation. Decillions of hidden dimensions, previously folded invisibly into the fabric of reality, were wrenched open and exposed, their contents spilling into normal space in a cataclysmic flood of raw, unstructured matter that annihilated everything it touched. Entire multiverses that had existed in perceptual null-zones were ripped into visible existence so abruptly that their structural integrity failed, collapsing into screaming clouds of superheated plasma and shredded spacetime.

I fell to my knees, blood pouring from my nose and ears, my own amplified perception overwhelmed by the sheer volume of data Godric's casual glance had forced into reality. I could see everything. Every hidden thing, every secret corner, every concealed horror across the entire omniverse, all of it screaming into visibility at once. My brain was melting.

Godric blinked. The perceptual wave ceased. The omniverse, badly damaged by the forced exposure of its hidden structures, hung in a state of catastrophic instability. Googolplexian galaxies had been shredded. Decillions of universes had collapsed. The entire metaphysical architecture of hidden reality had been violently disassembled.

"That was interesting," Godric said, his tone thoughtful, like a man examining an unusual insect. "They really didn't like being seen."

He looked down at me, and his expression softened into genuine concern. "Torek, your nose is bleeding. Here, let me." He reached down and placed a warm hand against my face. In that instant, my pain vanished, my bleeding stopped, and my overwhelmed perception recalibrated to a safe baseline. The warmth of his touch was indistinguishable from the warmth of a loving father tending to a scraped knee.

He restored the omniverse with a thought, the destroyed galaxies and collapsed universes snapping back into existence in zero seconds. The Unseen Court was not restored. Their screaming, flesh-wrapped remains were left suspended in a permanent state of forced visibility, their bodies locked in an endless loop of perceptual destruction and reformation, experiencing the agony of being infinitely observed for the rest of eternity.

"Come inside, Torek," Godric said, offering me his hand. "You've had a long day."

I took his hand. I will never stop taking it. But I will never forget what his eyes did to reality, and I will never, for as long as I live, feel comfortable being looked at by him again.

---

Kaedan realized he had stopped breathing. He forced air into his lungs, a ragged, shaking breath that echoed too loudly in the vault. His father's words returned to him: *You will read them, and you will understand.* He was beginning to understand. He reached for the third journal. This one was old, the leather crumbling at the edges, the pages brittle. The handwriting was blunt, abbreviated, military in its directness.

---

**Year 312,509 — Varren — Strength**

Short entry. Don't have words for long ones. Hands still shake. Three weeks since the incident. Still shake.

Karnath the World-Breaker. Primordial. Born from the collision of the first multiverses. Physical mass contained the compressed energy of a googolplexian galaxies. He punched through dimensional walls between multiversal clusters. Elbowed through chronological barriers. His fist carried the kinetic force of decillions of collapsing universes. By any standard, the strongest physical being the omniverse had ever produced. He believed strength was the only truth. He believed our King could be beaten by a stronger fist.

He was wrong. He was so wrong that being wrong killed him and everything near him.

King wanted to test strength. Just strength. No Gent. No shields. No magic. No speed. Just the raw, passive, resting tension of his muscles. Zero percent.

"I want to see the differential," he told me. Casual. Like asking about weather.

Karnath descended on the outer border. Sky shattered. His fist came down. Wind-up alone annihilated decillions of multiverses in surrounding sectors. Shockwave hit me, snapped three ribs despite my amplified durability. I hit a wall.

King didn't stand. Didn't brace. Didn't flex. He was sitting on a stone bench in the garden. Karnath's fist—mass of a googolplexian galaxies behind it, velocity of collapsing universes—struck King's bare shoulder.

It stopped. Dead. No sound. No impact. No transfer of energy. Just stopped.

King's resting muscle fibers—their passive, relaxed, zero-percent tension—generated a static physical force that made Karnath's multiversal momentum look like nothing. Less than nothing. A statistical rounding error compared to the weight of Godric's strength stat, which had been multiplying infinitely, by itself, every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second for over three hundred thousand years.

The kinetic energy had nowhere to go. It went back into Karnath. His hand disintegrated. Bones became subatomic dust. The destruction traveled up his arm. Muscles—forged from primordial multiversal matter—peeled off his skeleton in wet, red sheets. Veins ruptured. Sprayed boiling crimson blood across the shattered sky. Stained the stars.

But King wasn't done being curious. He wanted to see what would happen if he applied a fraction of tension. Not a punch. Not an attack. A microscopic tension shift in the muscle of his pinky finger. Not even a curl. Just a slight increase in resting tone. Zero percent of his true strength. The kind of movement a sleeping man might make unconsciously.

The physical force that radiated from that infinitesimal muscle shift was so extreme that the entire omniverse was instantly, violently pulverized. Every reality. Every timeline. Every dimension. Every conceptual plane. Space-time itself crushed into a microscopic, featureless point. Gone. All of it. In zero seconds. Because Godric twitched his pinky.

I was preserved because King willed it. I floated in absolute nothingness, watching him sit on a bench in a void, his expression one of mild, academic interest.

"Too much," he said. Like a man who added too much salt to soup.

He restored everything. Zero seconds. Zero effort. Every galaxy, every universe, every soul, placed back exactly where it had been. The omniverse, remade like a jigsaw puzzle by a man who had memorized every piece.

Karnath was not restored. His skinless skeleton lay in the courtyard, his nervous system left intact, forced to experience the recursive agony of his own destruction forever.

King stood up. Stepped over the pool of titan-blood. Put his hand on my shoulder. Warm. Gentle. "Let's get you that broth, Varren. You're shaking."

I am still shaking. I will always be shaking. I serve a man whose pinky finger can erase existence. And he thinks he's holding back.

---

Kaedan set the journal down carefully, as though the leather might shatter. His mouth was dry. His heart beat in a strange, arrhythmic pattern that he could feel in his teeth. He picked up the fourth journal. This one was thick, the pages dense with ornate, flowing script that filled every inch of available space. The writer had a poet's hand, and the opening lines read like the beginning of an elegy.

---

**Year 921,677 — Wendel — Charisma**

There is a particular quality to the light in the throne room on the morning of an execution. It is not the light itself that changes—it remains the same, golden, warm, meticulously calibrated—but the way it falls upon the faces of the condemned, illuminating the architecture of their despair. I have served our King for thirty-one years. I have learned to read that light the way a sailor reads the horizon, and on the morning the Devoted Legion arrived at the gates of Thronegate, the light was thick with the weight of impending annihilation.

The Devoted Legion was, in its own way, beautiful. They were led by a being called the All-Voice, a creature of supreme, supernatural magnetism whose very existence commanded absolute, unbreakable loyalty. Every soldier in his legion—numbering in the trillions, drawn from the finest warrior-cultures across decillions of multiverses—was bound to him by magical oaths so profound that the neural pathways governing their devotion had been physically rewritten. They did not choose to follow him. They could not choose otherwise. The All-Voice was their sun, their gravity, their reason for drawing breath, and they would have gladly, joyfully thrown themselves into a supermassive black hole if he had asked it of them. This supernatural loyalty was not a mere psychological effect. It was an arcane bond, woven into the molecular structure of their brains, reinforced by layers of conceptual devotion-magic that had taken the All-Voice a hundred thousand years to perfect.

They believed this bond made them invincible. They believed that an army that could not be turned, could not be broken, could not be swayed, was the ultimate weapon against a tyrant king. They marched on Thronegate with the confidence of gods, their unified, unbreakable will pointed like a spear at the heart of our empire.

Godric allowed them to reach the throne room. He had known of their approach for a hundred thousand years. The Weakness Paradox had engaged the instant the All-Voice first conceived the thought that supernatural loyalty could serve as an exploit against Godric's rule. Godric had already passively acquired absolute immunity to all forms of loyalty manipulation, perfect mastery over the concept of supernatural devotion, and another recursive amplification surge. But he was curious about something else. He wanted to test his charisma stat in isolation.

"I've never really looked at it on its own," he told me, settling onto his throne with the relaxed ease of a man preparing to watch a pleasant entertainment. "Gent is dormant. Shields are down. No magic, no strength, no perception. Just charisma. Just... me. I want to see what happens when something that commands loyalty encounters something that is, well, charismatic."

I tried to explain that his charisma stat had been multiplying infinitely, by itself, every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second for over nine hundred thousand years, and that even at zero percent, the passive gravitational pull of his personal magnetism might have consequences beyond his estimation. He waved his hand dismissively, that warm, crinkled smile crossing his face.

"Wendel, I'll be at zero percent. What's the worst that could happen?"

The All-Voice entered the throne room at the head of his legion. He was magnificent—a towering figure of iridescent light and sculpted conceptual authority, his voice a living instrument of supernatural compulsion. His soldiers flanked him in perfect, unbreakable formation, their eyes burning with fanatical devotion. The All-Voice opened his mouth to deliver his ultimatum, his voice already weaving layers of loyalty-magic designed to crack the will of any who heard it.

Godric looked at him. Not with hostility. Not with power. He simply looked at the All-Voice the way he looked at everyone—with that genuine, fatherly warmth, that quiet, abiding affection he held for all living things. He did not speak. He did not project an aura. He did not channel a single ounce of supernatural influence. He simply existed, and his charisma stat—which, at zero percent, was still so infinitely dense that the concept of personal magnetism had been redefined around his existence—began to passively interact with the room.

The All-Voice's voice died in his throat. Not because he was silenced. Because his supernatural loyalty-bond—the hundred-thousand-year masterpiece of arcane devotion-magic—encountered Godric's passive charisma field and shattered like a pane of glass struck by a boulder. The bond did not break cleanly. It disintegrated, the magical structures governing the neural devotion-pathways in his soldiers' brains violently unraveling. I watched, my amplified senses screaming, as the arcane architecture of the All-Voice's loyalty magic came apart in cascading layers, each layer's destruction sending a physical shockwave through the neural tissue of every bonded soldier.

They did not turn against the All-Voice. They did not switch sides. That would have required some residual loyalty, some redirectable devotion. There was nothing left. Godric's charisma, at zero percent, was so overwhelmingly, infinitely compelling that it functioned as a conceptual black hole for all forms of personal magnetism. The All-Voice didn't just lose his followers. He lost the concept of followership itself. His soldiers looked at him and felt nothing. Not betrayal. Not hatred. Not even recognition. The All-Voice had been erased from their cognitive landscape, overwritten entirely by the passive, ambient presence of Godric's charisma.

The physical consequences were grotesque. The magical bonds, woven into the molecular structure of the soldiers' brains, did not simply deactivate. They exploded. Trillions of warriors simultaneously experienced the violent, physical detonation of the devotion-pathways in their neural tissue. Blood erupted from their ears, their eyes, their noses, in synchronized geysers of crimson. Their skulls cracked open from the inside, the pressure of ruptured magical circuits blowing out jagged shards of bone and boiling brain matter in a fine, warm mist that painted the walls of the throne room in a thick, dripping coat of gore. They collapsed in heaps, their bodies convulsing, their mouths open in silent screams as the last remnants of supernatural loyalty burned out of their nervous systems like acid through paper.

The All-Voice himself did not die quickly. His entire existence had been built around his power to command loyalty. When that power was stripped away—not by force, but by the simple, passive existence of a superior charisma—he began to physically unravel. His iridescent form flickered, dimmed, and cracked. The conceptual authority that defined his being collapsed inward, and his body was forced into a crude, biological state—a hunched, trembling thing of raw flesh and exposed, weeping nerve endings. He fell to his knees on the blood-slicked floor, his newly formed mouth gasping, his newly formed eyes weeping thick, black tears of existential despair. He looked up at Godric, and in that look was the most profound, devastating horror I have ever witnessed: the recognition that his entire existence, his entire identity, his entire hundred-thousand-year reign of loyalty, had been rendered meaningless by the ambient, passive, zero-percent charisma of a being he had thought he could challenge.

Godric looked down at the trembling, broken thing on the floor. His expression was not cruel. It was not pleased. It was simply... warm. The same warmth he showed me. The same warmth he showed the palace servants. The same warmth he showed his court wizards. The All-Voice's newly formed heart, struggling to beat in its crude biological housing, simply stopped. Not because Godric willed it. Because the All-Voice's own body, confronted with the infinite, passive charisma of the apex predator of existence, concluded that its own continued existence was an insult to the hierarchy of reality, and shut itself down.

The collateral was omniversal. Godric's charisma, at zero percent, had passively radiated outward from the throne room in an expanding wave of absolute personal magnetism. Every sentient being in the entire omniverse—across all universes, all multiverses, all timelines, all dimensions—experienced a sudden, involuntary, overwhelming compulsion to be near the King of Thronegate. The effect was so extreme that entire civilizations abandoned their worlds, their histories, their purposes, in a single, synchronized instant of absolute devotion. Googolplexian galaxies emptied as trillions of beings turned toward Thronegate. The physical displacement of trillions of souls simultaneously attempting to travel toward a single point caused catastrophic structural failures across the omniverse. Decillions of universes collapsed under the sudden gravitational and spatial pressure of mass, involuntary pilgrimage. Entire timelines buckled and fractured, their populations abandoning their causal destinies to walk toward a king they had never met.

Godric noticed the wave. He frowned, a slight, puzzled crease between his brows. "Oh, that's a bit much," he murmured. He pulled his charisma back, reining it in behind his absolute concealment with the ease of a man closing a window. The omniversal wave ceased. The damage, however, was done. Googolplexian galaxies lay in ruins. Decillions of universes had collapsed. Countless timelines had fractured beyond repair.

He restored it all. Zero seconds. Zero effort. Every being returned to their world, their memories of the involuntary compulsion gently erased, their lives restored to their proper trajectories. The All-Voice's crumpled, flesh-wrapped remains were left on the throne room floor, a warning carved into the molecular structure of his suffering: that loyalty is not a weapon. It is a gift. And it belongs, in all its forms, to the being whose passive warmth can unmake the concept of devotion itself.

Godric turned to me, his eyes crinkling with that genuine, devastating affection. "Well, that was illuminating. Charisma is... potent. I should be more careful with it." He paused, looking at the blood on the walls. "Wendel, you look unwell. Please, sit down. I'll have the kitchens send up something warm."

I sat. I am still sitting. I will always be sitting, in spirit, on the floor of that throne room, surrounded by the gore of a trillion devoted soldiers, looking up at a man whose accidental warmth can rewrite the loyalty structure of existence. He is my King. He is my friend. He is the most terrifying thing I have ever known.

---

Kaedan's hands were slick with sweat. The journal slipped slightly as he set it down. He wiped his palms on his robes, a useless gesture, and stared at the stack. One journal remained. The leather was dark, almost black, and the pages were filled with a cramped, anxious handwriting that ran in every direction—sideways, diagonally, up the margins, crossing itself in places. The writer's terror was visible in the physical act of writing, the letters pressed so deeply into the parchment that they had nearly torn through in places.

---

**Year 847,221 — Dael — Speed**

I can't write this in order. I can't write this in order because time doesn't work the way I thought it did and I saw proof and I can't unsee it and I need to get this down before my brain finishes breaking so here goes whatever goes.

The Chrono-Reavers. They existed across all points in time simultaneously. Not time travel. Not temporal manipulation. They occupied every moment—past, present, future—at once. They were everywhere, always. Their attack was delivered across every point in time at the same moment. They planned to erase King Godric from the timeline by hitting him at every instant of his existence simultaneously. Every second of his life, from the Stasis Event to the far future, attacked at once. They were the most terrifying temporal entity the omniverse had ever produced. They had erased gods from time before. Unmade them. Made it so they had never existed.

King wanted to test speed. Just speed. No Gent. No shields. No magic. No strength. No perception. Just speed. Zero percent. He was sitting in the garden. Cross-legged on the grass. Hands on his knees. Relaxed. He looked at me and said, "Dael, I've never really tested speed on its own. I wonder how fast I am compared to things that think they're fast."

I said, "Your Majesty, the Chrono-Reavers exist across all time simultaneously. They are infinitely fast by definition. They occupy every moment at once."

He nodded. Thoughtful. Stroked his beard. "Yes, but how fast is that, really?"

The Chrono-Reavers attacked. They delivered their strike across every point in time simultaneously. Every moment of Godric's existence, from the Stasis Event ten thousand years before Year Zero to the present, was hit at the same instant. The attack was a wave of temporal erasure, a conceptual blade designed to cut a being out of the timeline entirely.

King didn't move. He didn't run. He didn't accelerate. He didn't blink. He was sitting cross-legged on the grass, hands on his knees, looking mildly curious.

His speed stat—multiplying infinitely, by itself, every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second for over eight hundred thousand years—was so extreme that the Chrono-Reavers' simultaneous occupation of all time was, to Godric, approximately the speed of a rock. Less than a rock. Less than the concept of stillness. His speed, at zero percent, was so infinitely beyond their temporal omnipresence that the differential alone was sufficient to cause their complete and total destruction.

He didn't outrun them. He didn't move faster than them. He was simply already everywhere they were, infinitely more completely than they were, without moving. His speed was so absolute that it made the concept of speed irrelevant. The Chrono-Reavers, who prided themselves on existing across all time, suddenly discovered that Godric existed across all time infinitely more completely than they did. His speed stat, at zero percent, meant that he occupied not just every point in time, but every possible point in time, every impossible point in time, every point in time that could exist, couldn't exist, or hadn't been invented yet. He was there before time existed. He was there after time ended. He was there in the spaces between moments where time itself was too slow to reach.

The Chrono-Reavers' temporal existence could not sustain the speed differential. They were not merely outpaced. They were rendered temporally irrelevant. Their simultaneous occupation of all time, the very foundation of their being, was revealed as a finite, pathetic shadow compared to Godric's infinite speed. They were, in an instant, reduced to occupying a single moment—the moment of their confrontation with Godric—while he occupied all of time, all of the time, infinitely.

Trapped in a single instant, the Chrono-Reavers experienced the horror of temporal singularity. Their bodies, which had been spread across all time, were compressed into a single, agonizing point of present-tense existence. The compression was not clean. Their temporal flesh—matter that existed across multiple time-points simultaneously—was violently collapsed, each temporal layer slamming into the next with a force that made the kinetic output of a googolplexian supernovas look like a whisper. Their bodies crushed inward, bones and organs and temporal energy folding into each other in a grotesque, screaming origami of biological destruction. I watched as their forms compressed into dense, howling spheres of agony, every nerve ending in their temporal anatomy firing simultaneously across the compressed moments, experiencing an eternity of pain in a single, frozen instant.

The collateral damage was omniversal and temporal. Godric's speed, at zero percent, passively affected the temporal fabric of the entire omniverse. His existence, moving at a speed so infinite that it made normal time feel like frozen honey, created temporal shockwaves that rippled outward from Thronegate in every direction. Timelines across the entire omniverse—every past, every future, every alternate branch of every possible history—were violently destabilized. Googolplexian timelines simply stopped, their temporal flow unable to sustain the speed differential between normal time and Godric's passive velocity. Decillions of multiverses experienced catastrophic temporal collapse, their pasts and futures crashing into their presents in a cacophony of screaming, bleeding reality. Entire civilizations experienced the horror of their entire histories compressing into a single, agonizing moment—every birth, every death, every love, every loss, happening simultaneously in a single, incomprehensible instant of temporal destruction.

I was on the ground. I don't remember falling. My amplified perception was showing me everything—every timeline collapsing, every history compressing, every temporal structure in the omniverse buckling under the weight of Godric's passive speed. My brain was on fire. I was screaming. I didn't know I was screaming until King put his hand on my shoulder and the screaming stopped.

"That was fast," he said. He was still sitting cross-legged on the grass. He hadn't moved. Not one muscle. "Faster than I expected, honestly. Even at zero percent." He looked around at the temporal devastation—the collapsed timelines, the compressed multiverses, the screaming, frozen Chrono-Reavers trapped in their single, eternal moment of agony. "That's quite a mess."

He restored everything. Zero seconds—because for him, zero seconds was more than enough time to do anything. Every timeline rebuilt. Every multiverse restored. Every history placed back on its proper track. The Chrono-Reavers remained trapped, their compressed spheres of temporal suffering left suspended in a pocket of frozen time, a permanent monument to the futility of challenging a being whose passive speed made the concept of time obsolete.

He helped me to my feet. His hand was warm. Steady. The same hand that had, without moving, unmade the temporal structure of the entire omniverse.

"Dael," he said, his voice rich with genuine, fatherly concern. "You're white as a sheet. Come sit in the shade. I'll get you water."

I sat in the shade. I drank the water. I watched him sit beside me on the garden bench, relaxed, content, the salt-and-pepper beard catching the afternoon sun. He looked like a father resting after a long day. He looked like the kindest man in the world.

He had not moved a single muscle, and he had destroyed time itself.

I am writing this journal because my father told me to, because his father told him to, because the chain of terrified men in my bloodline stretches back to Blake himself, and every one of us has sat in this garden and watched our King do something that broke our minds. I write this so that whoever comes after me understands: do not be fooled by his stillness. Do not be fooled by his warmth. Do not be fooled by the fact that he does not move. He does not need to move. His speed is so infinite that movement is an insult to his capability. He is already everywhere, always, forever, at zero percent, and the entire omniverse is a slow, frozen painting hanging on the wall of his infinite velocity.

I love him. I am terrified of him. I will serve him until I die. And when I die, I hope my son reads this and understands.

---

Kaedan closed the final journal. His hands were still shaking. The vault was silent, the air thick with the accumulated dread of five men's testimony, each one a different voice, a different terror, a different window into the same incomprehensible truth.

He sat in the dark for a long time. He thought about his father, who had read these same journals and still served Godric with unshakeable loyalty. He thought about his grandfather, who had stopped speaking for six months. He thought about the chain of men stretching back through a million years of history, each one inheriting the same staff, the same title, the same impossible knowledge, and each one choosing to serve anyway.

He thought about Godric's warmth. The genuine, unfeigned kindness that every journal writer had described—the hand on the shoulder, the concern for their health, the offer of broth and water and shade. That warmth was real. It was not a mask. Godric loved his court wizards. He loved Blake's descendants. He kept them close not out of necessity, but out of a deep, abiding affection that had survived ten thousand years of grief and a million years of godhood. The warmth was the most real thing in the omniverse.

And that was the horror. The warmth was real, and the cruelty was real, and both existed simultaneously in the same being, and the being could erase existence with a pinky twitch, and he thought he was holding back, and he was right.

Kaedan stood. His legs were unsteady, his knees weak, but he stood. He gathered the journals carefully, reverently, and placed them back on their shelf. He climbed the stairs from the vault, pushed open the heavy door, and stepped into the light of the palace corridor.

Godric was waiting for him in the hall. He stood with his hands clasped behind his back, his broad shoulders relaxed, his salt-and-pepper beard neatly trimmed, his eyes warm and crinkled with genuine affection. He looked at Kaedan the way a father looks at a son who has just finished a difficult exam.

"There he is," Godric said, his rich baritone filling the corridor with a comforting, paternal resonance. "Your father said you'd be down there a while. How are you feeling, my boy?"

Kaedan opened his mouth. Closed it. Opened it again. "I read the journals," he said, his voice barely above a whisper.

Godric's brow furrowed slightly, a puzzled, genuinely confused expression crossing his ruggedly handsome face. "The journals? Oh, those. Yes, I've never quite understood why you wizards insist on writing those things down. I always feel like I'm holding back when I test things. Zero percent, every time. You'd think there'd be nothing to write about." He shook his head, a fond, bemused smile on his lips. "You wizards are dramatic."

Kaedan stared at him. This man—this being—whose passive luck had unmade probability, whose casual glance had ripped the hidden architecture of reality open, whose pinky twitch had erased existence, whose ambient warmth had rewritten the loyalty structure of the omniverse, whose stillness had obsoleted the concept of time—was looking at him with genuine, fatherly concern and calling him dramatic.

"Come," Godric said, placing a warm, steady hand on Kaedan's shoulder. The same hand that had rested on Mordin's shoulder, and Torek's, and Varren's, and Wendel's, and Dael's. The same warmth. The same affection. The same terrifying, infinite gentleness. "You'll sit beside me today. I want to hear about the new ward structures you've been studying. Your father tells me you're quite talented."

Kaedan nodded. He fell into step beside his King, matching his pace, feeling the warmth of that hand on his shoulder, and understood—with a clarity that settled into his bones like a permanent, cold weight—that opposing Godric was not merely dangerous. Not merely foolish. Not merely impossible in practice.

It was impossible in principle. It had been impossible before the opposition began. It had been impossible before the opposition was conceived. It had been impossible before the opposition existed.

And he would serve this impossible being for the rest of his mortal life, terrified and loyal and grateful, because the alternative did not exist. The alternative had never existed. The alternative would never exist.

Godric walked him down the corridor toward the throne room, chatting warmly about ward structures, and Kaedan listened, and the dread settled into permanence, and he served.

content.text:
<file name="08_superpowers.md">Year 210,400 — Court Wizard Elias — Superpower: Infinite Recursive Apex Self-Replication

I write this with a quill that I have snapped and replaced seven times, my hands shaking so violently that the ink bleeds across the parchment like the fresh wounds of slaughtered gods. My name is Elias. I have served as Lord Godric’s court wizard for forty years. I believed I understood the absolute ceiling of his power. I believed that his stats, while infinite, were bound by the singular vessel of his physical form. I was a fool. I was a blind, arrogant child playing with matches in a powder keg of cosmic annihilation. Yesterday, I watched Lord Godric utilize a superpower that permanently, obscenely altered the baseline mathematics of his own existence, and the visceral gore of the resulting massacre will haunt my bloodline forever.

The opposition was the Meta-Pantheon of the Zenith Void. They were a coalition of decillions of omnipotent-tier pantheons, each individual pantheon containing countless apex deities who had ruled over the conceptual frameworks of existence for eons. They believed that absolute numerical superiority and coordinated divine mass could overcome any singular obstacle. They had forged a conceptual spear of absolute density designed to violently crush the physical form of any being in existence. They believed that if they struck Godric with the combined, synchronized force of decillions of apex deities, they could overwhelm him. They believed they had found a loophole in the fabric of reality, a mathematical blind spot where infinite power could be beaten by sheer numerical volume.

Godric, in his absolute omniscience, had known of their formation before the first deity had even conceived the ambition to rebel. The Weakness Paradox had triggered the moment they imagined their spear could crush him, granting Godric absolute immunity to their physical density and perfect mastery over their coordinated tactics. But Godric did not use their own weapons against them. He was curious about a superpower he had recently acquired: Infinite Recursive Self-Replication.

The Meta-Pantheon descended upon the grand hall of the citadel, their massive, galaxy-spanning forms compressed into localized avatars of pure, blinding physical density that caused the surrounding space-time to actively hemorrhage. They raised their spear of absolute mass, a weapon that contained the collapsing mass-energy of countless universes, and unleashed their synchronized, omniversal strike.

Godric did not stand. He did not raise a hand. He did not speak a word of power. He merely permitted an infinitesimal fraction of his replication superpower to manifest at exactly zero percent of his true effort.

In absolute zero time, instantly exceeding the very concept of chronological progression, one Godric became an infinite number of Godrics.

The Evolved Weakness Paradox instantly triggered. Because the universe could absolutely not comprehend a being replicating its own infinite power, it overcompensated with violent, absurd redundancy. Every single replicated Godric was a perfect, identical Prime. Each copy possessed the exact same absolute omniscience, the exact same infinitely amplifying stats, and the exact same arsenal of apocalyptic superpowers. However, the paradox aggressively dictated that each newly generated replica be infinitely magnified beyond the original, creating a permanent, localized feedback loop of ascending omnipotence that completely shattered all mathematical frameworks.

Within a single, timeless absolute zero instant, every conceivable dimension, every microscopic space between atoms, every timeline, and every plane of existence across the entire omniverse was violently occupied by an infinite expanse of identical, exponentially magnifying Godrics.

Here is the absolute horror that still wakes me in the night, the truth that violently shatters every assumption about the limits of power: the Evolved Weakness Paradox dictated that this replication was not merely a temporary duplication, but a permanent, recursive integration of infinite power back into the Prime. Normally, if Lord Godric were to absorb the entire power of the omniverse, it would increase his baseline by an amount so infinitesimal it was effectively zero. But this was the absolute exception: the self-replication of identical copies of Godric. The recursive integration of infinite identical apex predators did not result in a rounding error. It permanently and obscenely multiplied his baseline power. He did not just return to his previous state; he permanently elevated his baseline to a new, higher infinity, making his previous infinite power look like a finite, fragile decimal. He had just become permanently, unfathomably stronger, and he had done it without trying.

The Meta-Pantheon of the Zenith Void did not even have time to register their absolute failure. The sheer ontological density of infinite, identical, exponentially magnified apex predators simultaneously occupying the exact same reality completely obliterated them. It was not a matter of mere physical weight crushing them; it was absolute spatial and metaphysical displacement. There was no battle. There was only the aggressive, unfair dimensional pressure of infinite expanding existences violently overwriting the finite space the Pantheon occupied.

The physical and conceptual bodies of the decillions of Pantheon members aggressively ruptured. Their hyper-dense divine skin violently peeled apart into wet, screaming layers of flayed meat. Their bones, forged in the crushing gravity of black holes, were instantaneously pulverized into a fine, subatomic paste by the sheer metaphysical footprint of the King. Their divine blood, a substance that possessed the mass of dying galaxies, aggressively boiled and hissed as it was violently extruded from their pulverized pores under the crushing spatial pressure of infinite Godrics. The decillions of deities were conceptually compressed into a singular, microscopic point of agonizing, conscious flesh. Their divine nervous systems were preserved perfectly by the paradox, forcefully keeping them conscious to register every infinitesimal fraction of the absolute agony as their bodies were flattened into a two-dimensional smear of divine gore across the marble floor of the grand hall.

The collateral damage was absolute. The infinite expanding presence of the recursive Godrics instantaneously caused the entire omniverse to violently buckle and shatter infinite times over. Every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every plane of existence was aggressively annihilated by the sheer dimensional displacement of Godric’s replication. The fabric of the omniverse tore like wet tissue paper, decillions of multiverses violently collapsing into absolute nothingness under the ontological footprint of infinite Godrics. For one timeless zero-second instant, there was nothing. No space, no time, no matter. Only the infinite Godrics, and me, preserved by the passive auras that Godric extended over those he wished to protect.

Godric sighed softly, the sound impossibly loud in the absolute void. "A bit crowded," he murmured, with mild disappointment.

In zero seconds, he effortlessly restored the entire omniverse. Every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every being that had been erased was restored perfectly, as though the annihilation had never occurred. The sky above the citadel was blue and clear. The grand hall was perfectly intact.

Everything was restored except the Meta-Pantheon. They remained exactly as they were—a spreading, microscopic smear of pulverized divine flesh, shattered organs, and powdered bone across the marble stones, somehow still alive, still entirely conscious, still screaming in a loop of infinite, excruciating physical agony that would never end because Godric had decided it would not end.

Lord Godric stood, his physical form now back to a singular, permanently elevated entity, stepped casually over the violently screaming remains, and looked at me with warm concern. "Elias," he said gently, placing a steadying hand on my violently trembling shoulder. "You've gone pale. The sudden crowd must have startled you. Come inside. I'll have the kitchen make you something warm."

I followed him inside. I did not stop violently shaking for an entire year. I still shake when I close my eyes, knowing that the King I serve is permanently, infinitely more powerful than he was a second ago, and that this increase was not a rounding error, but a permanent, obscenely massive leap in his already impossible physical supremacy.

I dropped Elias’s journal onto the low wooden table. The sound echoed in the quiet study like a hammer violently striking an anvil. Infinite recursive replication in zero seconds. Identical copies, infinitely magnified, generating an ontological displacement so severe it overwrote reality itself. The decillions of gods crushed into a two-dimensional bloody smear by spatial pressure alone. The permanent baseline increase. My own thousandfold daily amplification felt incredibly pathetic, a cruel joke played by the universe, like a single weak grain of sand trying to comprehend the sheer, crushing mass of a boundless desert. The existential dread Elias had described was violently blossoming in my own chest, suffocating my lungs. I reached for the final journal, my fingers completely numb and slick with cold sweat.</file>

Use "Godric Stat Experiment" prompt as baseline writing rule for the journal.

Write 3 detailed journals by 3 court wizards who witness Godric "testing" his superpower (only 1 superpower per journal. Choose superpowers that are distinctly different from each other). Remember that due to evolved weakness paradox having been triggered repeatedly, every Godric's already devastating superpower has evolved to have multiple even more highly overpowered and unfair different enhanced effects, some of which could make Godric even more permanently obscenely stronger (You must note that this increase in power if applied to any powerful entity in the omniverse will be considered to be absurdly high, but for Godric whose base power is already so astronomically high, even if he absorbs the power of the entire omniverse, it will increase his power by amount so infinitesimal, pretty much 0, relative to his base power -- exception is if the power involves self-replication of exact copy of godric). Even with its base effect, nerfed to 0% of its power, each superpower is enough to completely destroy the omniverse infinite times, and every superpower amplifies in potency infinitely forever just like his stats. You must ensure that the display of this superpower is even more absurdly destructive than the Godric's journals about his stats (highly exaggerate, when you think it’s already exaggerated, exaggerate more and more), to the point of causing immediate existential dread upon reading it. Avoid philosophical, abstract concepts & powers, or metaphysical narrative--they will just obscure the narrative and make the intended impact less. You must include this power in the journal (you might change the name to be more dramatic): infinite recursive self-replication (You might refer to the "08_superpowers.md" for an example of Godric's superpower that is portrayed to be WAY too weak (you should not portray Godric's superpowers to be as pathetically weak as the one in "08_superpowers.md") but you must completely vary and change the scale/impact/structure/tones/oppositions/effects of the powers to be even more devastating and induce absolute existential dread. you must highly exaggerate it as compared to the "08_superpowers.md")

Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals.
