content:
I found Osvald's second journal behind the first. It was not on the shelf where it should have been. It was wedged between the stone blocks of the vault wall, as though Osvald had not merely stored it but hidden it—buried it in the architecture of the palace itself, as though the account it contained was not merely terrifying but structurally dangerous, a document so volatile that it required the physical mass of Thronegate's foundation stones to keep it contained. I had to use a localized spatial-compression spell to extract it without damaging the wall. The leather was warm. Not body-warm. Not ambient-temperature warm. Warm the way a compressed marble of infinite omniversal mass is warm—with the deep, persistent, internal heat of something that contained more energy than it should, that held inside its pages an account of something that had produced more existential pressure than the vault's stone could absorb.

I opened it. The handwriting was different from Osvald's first journal. The first journal had been shaky, the writing of a man processing trauma in real time. This journal was steady. Unnervingly steady. The letters were precise, controlled, each one formed with the deliberate, almost mechanical accuracy of a man who had moved past trembling and arrived at a place beyond physical expression of fear—a place where the fear was so total, so structural, so integrated into the writer's being that his hands had stopped shaking because shaking was a response to threat, and there was no response adequate to what he had witnessed, so the body had simply stopped trying.

---

**Year [Redacted — Osvald wrote only "After the Marble"] — Osvald, Age 44 — The Creator's Challenge**

I need to state, before anything else, that I did not believe the encounter was real. I believed, for the first several moments, that I was experiencing a hallucination produced by the residual psychic trauma of the Marble Incident, which had occurred a short time before—I will not specify how short, because time had become a fluid and unreliable concept in the aftermath of that event, and I am no longer certain that my perception of chronological sequence corresponds to any objective reality, given that the reality I inhabit is a copy and the original is a marble in my King's pocket. But I believed I was hallucinating, because what I was seeing could not be happening. The being that appeared before the palace could not exist. The being that appeared before the palace was God.

Not a god. Not one of the countless deities, cosmic entities, divine hierarchies, or transcendent consciousnesses that populated the omniverse's religious and metaphysical frameworks. Those were common. Those were, by Thronegate's standards, administrative categories—beings of sufficient power to be classified as divine, but fundamentally no different from any other entity in the hierarchy of existence, occupying positions on the spectrum that Godric had long since transcended. I had met gods. I had watched gods garden. I had watched gods polish furniture. Gods were staff.

This was not that. This was God. The God. The creator. The being that, according to every theological framework in every reality across the entire omniverse, had authored existence itself. The origin. The first cause. The uncaused cause. The being that had, by varying accounts across countless civilizations and cosmic hierarchies, spoken the first word, ignited the first spark, drawn the first line, conceived the first thought, and set into motion the chain of events that had produced everything—every particle, every force, every law, every dimension, every reality, every timeline, every being, every consciousness, every story, every fiction, every possibility, every impossibility, every everything. Including, I realized with a cold, dense, sinking weight in my sternum, Godric.

God had created Godric. Not directly, perhaps. Not intentionally, perhaps. But the chain of causation was clear: God had created existence, existence had produced the universe containing Olympus, Olympus had produced the mortal man Godric, and the mortal man Godric had been transformed—through the Stasis Event, through his future self's intervention, through the amplification and the superpowers and the mechanisms—into the being sitting on the oak throne in the palace behind me. Godric was, in the most literal, most theological, most cosmologically accurate sense, a creation of God. A product. A thing made by the maker. And the maker had arrived.

I need to describe God, because the description matters. God was not what I expected. I had no clear expectations—I am a court wizard, not a theologian—but whatever vague, culturally-inherited image I carried of the divine, it did not match the being that stood before the palace. God was not a bearded man on a throne. God was not a burning bush, a pillar of light, a cosmic consciousness, or an abstract mathematical principle. God was—everything. Not metaphorically. Literally. God's form, if it could be called a form, was a presence that occupied the entirety of visible space without displacing any of it. The sky was God. The ground was God. The air between the sky and the ground was God. The light that allowed me to see was God. The neurons that allowed me to process the light were God. God was not in everything. God was everything, and everything was God, and the distinction between the two had been dissolved by the sheer, total, absolute nature of the divine presence.

And the presence was powerful. I could feel it—not through my magical senses, which were calibrated for arcane phenomena, but through something deeper, something more fundamental, something that existed beneath my magical perception and beneath my physical senses and beneath my cognitive processing, in the foundational layer of my being where the basic fact of my existence was registered. God's power was not energy. It was not magic. It was not force. It was authority. The authority of the creator over the created. The authority of the author over the story. The authority of the origin over the derivative. It was the fundamental, structural, axiomatic authority of the being that had made everything over the everything it had made, and the authority was absolute, and the authority was vast, and the authority was, by any standard that was not Godric—nothing.

I knew this because I was standing beside Godric when God arrived, and Godric's reaction was not fear. It was not awe. It was not reverence. It was not respect. It was not even acknowledgment, in the formal sense. Godric's reaction was amusement. Genuine, warm, delighted amusement, the amusement of a man who has just seen an old friend walk through the door unexpectedly. His salt-and-pepper beard shifted as a broad, crinkled smile spread across his ruggedly handsome face. His heavily muscled frame relaxed further into the oak throne—not tensing, not preparing, not activating any defensive system. Relaxing. The way a man relaxes when something entertaining has happened.

"Oh," Godric said, his rich baritone carrying the pleased, surprised tone of a man who has just received an unexpected gift. "Well, this is interesting."

He looked at me. His eyes were warm, crinkled, filled with that genuine, paternal affection that was the most terrifying thing about him. "Osvald," he said, "do you know who that is?"

I said I did.

"That's God," Godric said, with the same tone a man uses when identifying a bird in a garden. "The creator. The origin. The—" He paused, tilting his head, his omniscient awareness processing something that I could not perceive. "The author of the framework. The being that drew the first line and wrote the first rule and set the first parameter. The being that, in a very real and technically accurate sense, made me." He smiled again. The smile was warm. "I haven't seen Him in—well, I've always seen Him. I'm omniscient. But I haven't been seen by Him in—well, He's always seen me. He's God. He sees everything." The smile widened. "But He's here. In person. That's new."

God spoke. I will attempt to describe the experience of God speaking, with the caveat that no mortal language was designed to convey it. When God spoke, the words were not sounds. They were revisions. Each word that God uttered was a rewrite of the fundamental rules of existence, a direct edit to the source code of reality, applied instantaneously and absolutely across every framework, every dimension, every timeline, every possible and impossible permutation of existence. God did not say words. God said truths, and the truths became real by virtue of having been said, because God's speech and reality were the same thing, and the thing that God said, in its entirety, was a declaration of authority over Godric.

The declaration was, as far as I could comprehend it, a claim of ownership. God stated—through the reality-revising mechanism of His speech—that Godric, as a created being, as a product of the existence that God had authored, was subject to God's authority. That the Stasis Event, while created by Godric's future self, had occurred within a framework that God had established. That the amplification, the superpowers, the mechanisms—all of them operated within the parameters of the reality that God had designed. That Godric, no matter how powerful, existed inside God's creation, and the creator had authority over the creation.

It was, by any theological or philosophical standard, a valid claim. It was the claim of the potter over the clay. The claim of the author over the character. The claim of the programmer over the code. It was the most fundamental, most axiomatic, most structurally sound claim of authority that could be made, and it was being made by the being that had the absolute, unquestionable right to make it.

Godric laughed.

Not a cruel laugh. Not a mocking laugh. A warm, genuine, delighted laugh. The laugh of a man who has heard a very good joke. The laugh echoed through the palace, through the compressed dimensional folds of the throne room, through the marble pocket where the compressed original omniverse sat and screamed and amplified. It was the laugh of a being who had been challenged by his own creator and found the challenge funny.

"That's wonderful," Godric said, wiping his eye with a calloused thumb, his salt-and-pepper beard still trembling with residual mirth. "That's genuinely wonderful. He made me. He made everything. And now He's here, in person, to—" He shook his head, still smiling. "To claim authority. Over me. The potter over the clay." He looked at me, and his eyes were bright with amusement. "Osvald, do you understand? He's the potter. And I'm the clay. And the clay is—" The smile widened. "The clay is so far beyond the potter that the metaphor has collapsed."

He straightened on his throne. His heavily muscled frame settled into an upright posture, his broad shoulders squaring, his calloused hands resting on his knees. His expression shifted—not hardening, not becoming cruel, but focusing. The warm amusement remained, but beneath it, something else surfaced. Playfulness. The specific, light, mischievous playfulness that I had read about in the journals of my predecessors, the playfulness that Godric exhibited when he was about to allow an opponent to attempt something, the playfulness that preceded demonstrations so one-sided, so absolute, so cosmically unfair that the word "demonstration" was an insult to the word.

"Gent," Godric said, his voice conversational, carrying the tone of a man giving a casual instruction to a household staff member. "Stand down."

The air in the throne room did not change. Nothing visible happened. But I felt it—a subtle, imperceptible withdrawal, the removal of a presence I had not consciously registered because it had been so constant, so ambient, so fundamental to the environment that its absence was like the absence of gravity. Gent, Godric's weakest sentient passive aura, his waste heat, his excess body heat given awareness and purpose—the aura that could slaughter omniversal coalitions while infinitely nerfed—was gone. Not suppressed. Not reduced. Gone. Standing down. Obeying its master's command with the instant, absolute, unquestioning obedience of a being whose entire existence was defined by loyalty.

"All of you," Godric added, and this was directed not at Gent but at the others—the countless other sentient passive auras, the evolved siblings, the ones that had grown so powerful that Gent, even in its current evolved state, was a rounding error among them. The ones that slept in dormancy because their clingy subservience annoyed him. "Stand down. All of you. I want to feel this."

The withdrawal intensified. Every sentient passive aura—every waste-heat byproduct, every excess emanation, every evolved, adapted, omniscient, omnipresent fragment of Godric's passive defense system—deactivated. Not suppressed. Deactivated. They obeyed. They were absolutely subordinate to his will, and his will was that they not exist for the duration of this encounter, and so they did not exist.

"Shields," Godric said. "Down. All of them. Every layer. Every recursive iteration. Every passive, pre-emptive, conceptual, metaphysical, logical, causal, and axiomatic shield. Deactivate."

I felt this one. The air in the throne room changed. Not dramatically—not the way it would have changed if the shields had dropped without Godric's concealment holding, which would have erased the omniverse. But subtly, structurally, in the way the foundational physics of the space shifted when the infinite recursive layers of passive protection that surrounded Godric's body like concentric walls around a fortress were, one by one, voluntarily taken offline. The shields that made the concept of threat logically impossible. The shields that converted attempted danger into self-erasing failure. The shields that rewrote causality so that opposition required its own nonexistence. All of them. Deactivated. By choice. By a casual, playful command, delivered in the tone of a man turning off a security system because he wants to see what happens.

Godric sat on his oak throne, in the compressed throne room, with no sentient auras, no passive shields, no defensive mechanisms of any kind active. Only his body. Only his stats. Only his concealment, which remained—not because he needed it, but because removing it would erase the omniverse, and he did not want the omniverse erased right now, because he wanted to watch the show.

He looked at God. God looked at Godric. The creator looked at the creation. The potter looked at the clay.

"Alright," Godric said, his baritone warm, conversational, carrying the easy, unhurried tone of a man settling in to watch a pleasant entertainment. "Go ahead."

God attacked.

I will attempt to describe what happened next, with the explicit caveat that my amplified perception, which could track the quantum state of every particle in a galactic cluster simultaneously, was able to perceive approximately the first infinitesimal fraction of the first instant of the attack before the scale of what was occurring exceeded my capacity to process. What follows is my best reconstruction, supplemented by Godric's subsequent, casual, offhand description of the event, which he provided while drinking tea.

God's attack was not an attack in any conventional sense. It was not a beam, or a blast, or a strike, or a spell, or a conceptual overwrite, or a narrative revision, or a metaphysical imposition. It was a deletion. God, as the author of existence, exercised the authority of the author: He attempted to delete Godric from the story. Not kill. Not destroy. Delete. The way a writer deletes a character from a manuscript—highlighting the text and pressing the backspace key, removing the character not just from the current scene but from the entire narrative, from every chapter, from every reference, from every memory, from every consequence, from every causal chain, so that the character had never existed, would never exist, and could never exist, and the story would continue as though the character had never been written.

The deletion was absolute. God did not merely target Godric's physical form. He targeted Godric's existence—every particle, every energy state, every conceptual framework, every dimensional anchor, every timeline reference, every causal link, every narrative thread, every metaphysical category, every possible and impossible permutation of the being called Godric. The deletion extended backward through time, erasing Godric from every moment he had ever occupied. It extended forward through time, erasing Godric from every moment he would ever occupy. It extended sideways through dimensions, erasing Godric from every parallel reality and alternate timeline. It extended into the abstract, erasing the concept of Godric from every framework that could conceive of him. It extended into the nonexistent, erasing the possibility of Godric from every void that could potentially give rise to him. It was the most comprehensive, most absolute, most total act of erasure that could be conceived by the being whose conception had defined conception itself.

The deletion struck Godric.

Godric did not move. He did not react. He did not activate a defense, because he had deactivated all of his defenses. He did not channel a stat, because he was using zero percent of his stats. He did not express a superpower, because he was using zero percent of his superpowers. He sat on his oak throne, hands on his knees, salt-and-pepper beard catching the light, and the deletion—the absolute, omniversal, creator-level deletion of a being from the entirety of existence—struck his body and did nothing.

Nothing.

Not "nothing" in the sense of "he resisted it." Not "nothing" in the sense of "he was too powerful to be affected." Nothing in the sense that the deletion arrived at Godric's location and found that there was nothing to delete. Not because Godric was not there. He was there. He was sitting on his throne, breathing, existing, being. But the deletion—the creator's authority to remove a created being from existence—could not find a target. Not because Godric was hidden. Not because Godric was shielded. Because Godric's existence was not the kind of thing that could be deleted. The deletion was a narrative act—the removal of a character from a story. And Godric was not a character. He was not a thing written by the author. He was—the only way I can describe this, and the description is inadequate, and the inadequacy is itself a form of terror—the paper. The deletion was an eraser. The eraser was applied to the paper. And the paper did not erase, because the paper was not written. The paper was the medium. The medium could not be deleted by the author, because the author existed on the medium, and deleting the medium would delete the author, and the author could not delete the author, because the author's ability to delete was a function of the medium, and the medium was Godric.

This is not accurate. This metaphor is wrong. I know it is wrong because Godric later explained, casually, over tea, that what had actually happened was simpler than my metaphor and more horrifying. He said: "The deletion hit me and I didn't feel it." That was his explanation. Not that he was the paper. Not that he existed outside the narrative. Not that his existence was metaphysically prior to God's authorship. He didn't feel it. The deletion—the most comprehensive act of erasure conceivable by the creator of existence—struck his body, at zero percent of his power, with zero percent of his effort, with all defenses deactivated, and he did not feel it. Not "it didn't affect him." He did not feel it. The deletion did not register as a stimulus. It did not register as a non-stimulus. It did not register. The interaction between God's deletion and Godric's body produced no measurable, detectable, or conceptual interaction of any kind. The deletion was there. Godric was there. And the two did not meet, because the deletion existed on a scale so far below Godric's body that the concept of meeting did not apply. The deletion was a thing that existed within the framework of creation. Godric's body, at rest, at zero percent, with no defenses, was so vastly, so incomprehensibly, so absurdly more fundamental than the framework of creation that the deletion's operation within the framework could not reach him. He was not outside the framework. He was beneath it. He was the ground the framework stood on. The deletion could not reach the ground because the deletion was a feature of the framework, and features of the framework did not extend to the ground, and the ground was Godric, and Godric was sitting on a throne, drinking tea, and the ground did not feel the framework's operations.

Godric looked at God. God looked at Godric. The silence that followed was the loudest silence I have ever experienced, because the silence was filled with the sound of the creator of existence processing the fact that His most absolute, most fundamental, most axiomatic act of authority—the deletion of a created being from the story He had written—had failed. Not been resisted. Not been deflected. Not been overpowered. Failed. The way a pen fails to write on water. Not because the water resists the pen. Because the pen was designed for paper, and the surface it encountered was not paper, and the pen's function did not apply to the surface, and the surface did not care about the pen.

Godric took a sip of tea. He set the cup down on the armrest of his oak throne. He looked at God with those warm, crinkled, devastating eyes, and he chuckled. A low, warm, genuine chuckle. The chuckle of a man who has just watched a child try to pick up a boulder. The chuckle was not cruel. It was not mocking. It was the chuckle of genuine, paternal amusement at the gap between effort and result, between intent and outcome, between the desire to move something and the something's absolute, structural, axiomatic immobility.

"That was very thorough," Godric said, his baritone carrying the warm, appreciative tone of a man complimenting a chef on a well-prepared meal. "Really. The scope of it. The ambition. You went for everything—past, present, future, conceptual, narrative, causal, metaphysical. The full deletion. The complete erasure. I've seen a lot of attacks, and that was—" He nodded with genuine respect. "That was comprehensive."

He paused. He picked up his teacup. He took another sip.

"It didn't tickle," he said.

Three words. Three words that contained within them the total, absolute, final collapse of the distinction between creator and creation, between author and character, between potter and clay. Three words spoken casually, over tea, by a forty-five-year-old man sitting on an oak throne, to the being that had authored existence itself. It didn't tickle. The most comprehensive act of erasure conceivable by the creator of everything had not produced a tickle. Not a small tickle. Not a faint tickle. Not the memory of a tickle. Not the concept of a tickle. Nothing. The absolute, total, categorical absence of sensation. The creator's maximum output, delivered against the creation's body at zero percent with no defenses, had produced less than nothing. It had produced nothing. And the nothing was funny, and Godric chuckled, and the chuckle was warm.

"You know," Godric said, setting his teacup down again, his tone conversational, reflective, carrying the quiet, private quality of a man sharing a personal observation, "I was thinking about this earlier, and I realized something. The me that existed at Year 0—the me that had just awakened from stasis, the me that had been omnipotent for approximately five minutes, the me that was, by my current standard, so pathetically, so cosmically, so hilariously weak that the comparison is not merely unfavorable but structurally impossible—" He paused, stroking his beard. "That me could have annihilated you."

He said this to God. To the creator. To the being that had authored existence. He said it casually, the way a man mentions a minor historical fact. The Year 0 Godric—the baby, the seed, the primitive, the version of himself that had been omnipotent for five minutes and had not yet experienced any of the amplification, any of the superpower evolution, any of the aura births, any of the immune system development, any of the passive shield crystallization, any of the Evolved Weakness Paradox, any of the countless evolutionary iterations that had transformed him from "merely" the most powerful being in the omniverse into Godric Prime—that Godric, at zero percent, with zero effort, could have annihilated God. Not defeated. Not overpowered. Annihilated. The creator of existence, the author of everything, the being whose speech was reality-revising and whose will was axiomatic, could have been annihilated by the weakest, most primitive, most pathetically underdeveloped version of the being sitting on the oak throne.

"And I'm not talking about a fight," Godric continued, his tone still conversational, still warm, still carrying the easy, unhurried cadence of a man discussing the weather. "There would be no fight. The Year 0 me—the baby me, the weak me—would not have needed to fight. He would not have needed to attack. He would not have needed to activate a single mechanism. He would have simply existed, and the existing, at zero percent, with zero effort, would have been sufficient. His passive body heat—the waste energy radiating from his skin at rest, the thermal output of a man sitting still—would have been enough. You, the creator, the author of existence, would have encountered the passive body heat of the weakest version of me, and the encounter would have been fatal. Not because the body heat was hot. Because the body heat existed on a scale so fundamentally beyond your operational parameters that the concept of temperature did not apply, and your existence—your divine, creative, axiomatic existence—would have been unable to sustain itself in the presence of something so vastly more fundamental than itself."

He looked at God. The creator was still. The stillness was not the stillness of calm. It was the stillness of a being whose entire framework of self-understanding had just been dismantled. God had created everything. God had authored existence. God was the origin, the first cause, the uncaused cause. And the creation—a being that God had made, indirectly, through the chain of causation that began with the creation of existence—was sitting on a throne and explaining, casually, over tea, that the weakest version of itself could have annihilated the creator by passively existing. That the creator's maximum output—the comprehensive deletion of a being from existence—had not tickled. That the gap between creator and creation was not a gap in degree but a gap in category, and the category the creator occupied was "nothing" compared to the category the creation occupied, and the "nothing" was not a metaphor.

I watched God's reaction. I watched because I needed to see it, because the reaction of the creator of existence to the revelation of its own absolute, categorical insignificance was something that no mortal was meant to witness, and witnessing it was a privilege and a curse and a form of psychological damage that would persist through every subsequent moment of my existence.

God experienced existential dread. Not the existential dread of a mortal confronting death. Not the existential dread of a god confronting a greater god. The existential dread of the author confronting the realization that the character has become the paper. The existential dread of the origin confronting the realization that the derivative has transcended the source. The existential dread of the creator confronting the realization that the creation does not need the creator, has never needed the creator, and is so vastly, so incomprehensibly, so absurdly more powerful than the creator that the creator's existence is a rounding error in the creation's passive ambient output.

The dread manifested physically. God's presence—the total, omnipresent, everything-is-God presence that had filled the sky and the ground and the air and the light—contracted. The creator of existence, confronting the absolute insignificance of its own authority, pulled inward. The sky became less God. The ground became less God. The air became less God. The divine presence, which had been total and absolute, retreated, compressing itself, diminishing, as though the creator was trying to make itself smaller, to take up less space, to be less present in the vicinity of the being that had just told it, over tea, that it was nothing.

Godric watched the contraction with mild, paternal interest. "You're shrinking," he observed, his tone carrying the gentle, nonjudgmental quality of a man noting a weather change. "That's understandable. I would be unsettled too, if I were in your position." He paused. "Well, no. I wouldn't. I can't be in your position. Your position is—" He gestured vaguely at the contracting divine presence. "Below me. By a margin that the word 'below' is too generous to describe."

He finished his tea. He set the cup down. He looked at God with those warm, crinkled, devastating eyes, and his expression shifted—not hardening, not becoming cruel, but settling into a stillness that was, I understood, the end of the playfulness. The game was over. The show was done. God had attacked, and the attack had not tickled, and the explanation had been given, and the dread had been witnessed, and now the encounter was reaching its conclusion, and the conclusion was the same conclusion that every encounter with Godric reached: the opposition ceased to exist.

"It was nice to meet you," Godric said, and his voice was warm, and the warmth was genuine, and the genuine warmth was the most terrifying thing in the omniverse. "I mean that sincerely. You made me. Not directly, not intentionally, but the chain of causation starts with you, and I am a link in the chain, and meeting the first link has been—" He paused, searching for the word. "Charming. You are charming. Your attack was comprehensive. Your presence is—was—impressive. And your dread is—" Another pause. "Understandable."

He did not gesture. He did not speak a word of power. He did not channel a stat, or express a superpower, or activate a mechanism. He simply existed, and the existing, at zero percent, with zero effort, with all defenses deactivated and all auras standing down and all shields offline, did what it always did when Godric's existence was expressed without restraint in the vicinity of something that existed on a lesser scale: it annihilated.

God was de-atomized. The process was instantaneous from Godric's perspective—a fraction of time so small that calling it a fraction was generous—and eternal from God's perspective, because Godric's passive existence, interacting with the divine presence, subjected God to the same compressive, de-atomizing force that had annihilated the Amalgamation and the entire omniverse during the Marble Incident. God's form—the omnipresent, everything-is-God presence that had filled the sky and the ground and the air—was compressed. The sky stopped being God. The ground stopped being God. The air stopped being God. The divine presence, which had been everything, was squeezed into a smaller and smaller volume, the compression so total, so absolute, so fundamentally overwhelming that God's conceptual framework—the framework of the creator, the axiomatic structure that had authored existence—came apart.

The framework did not shatter. Shattering implies resistance. The framework dissolved. The way a sugar cube dissolves in water—not because the water attacks the sugar, but because the sugar's structural integrity is insufficient to maintain itself in the medium. God's framework—the divine architecture that had authored existence, that had defined the rules by which reality operated, that had established the parameters within which everything, including Godric, had been produced—encountered Godric's passive existence and dissolved. The rules were unwritten. The parameters were undefined. The axioms were unaxiomed. God's authority—the authority of the creator over the created, the authority of the author over the story—ceased to function, not because it was overpowered, but because the medium in which the authority operated—the medium of existence, the medium of reality, the medium of everything—was Godric, and Godric's existence did not support the operation of authorities lesser than itself.

God's consciousness—the awareness of the creator, the mind that had conceived existence, the first thought—was compressed into the same undifferentiated, screaming state as the Amalgamation, as the omniverse, as everything that had ever been compressed by Godric's passive existence. The creator's awareness was forced into a point smaller than a quark, every divine thought merged with every other divine thought, every creative act merged with every other creative act, the entire history of creation—the first word, the first spark, the first line, the first rule—compressed into a single, infinite, screaming instant of maximum agony.

The collateral damage was omniversal. Again. Godric's passive existence, expressed without restraint—even at zero percent, even with zero effort, even with nothing more than the ambient, resting, body-heat-level output of a man sitting on a throne—was so vastly more powerful than the framework of existence that the framework could not sustain itself in his presence. The omniverse—the restored copy, the reconstruction, the reality that every being in existence inhabited and believed was the totality of everything—was subjected to the same compressive force that had annihilated God. Every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every plane, every void, every nonexistent nowhere—all of it, the absolute totality of the copy—was de-atomized, compressed, and squeezed into a point smaller than a quark.

The process was identical to the Marble Incident. The omniverse came apart. Every star was crushed. Every planet was de-atomized. Every being was compressed into a screaming point of undifferentiated consciousness. The entire, absolute, total everything of the copy was reduced to a point, and the point was screaming, and the screaming was eternal, and the eternal was a fraction of a Planck second, and the fraction was zero, and zero was the time it took Godric to annihilate everything by existing.

Godric sat in the nothing. The oak throne was there—I was there, preserved by his will—and the nothing was around us, and the nothing was total, and in the nothing was a point smaller than a quark that contained the compressed, screaming mass of the creator of existence and the entire omniverse that the creator had authored.

Godric reached into the nothing and picked up the point. He held it between his thumb and index finger. He examined it with mild interest. Then he reached into his tunic pocket and produced the marble—the original marble, from the Amalgamation Incident, containing the compressed, screaming, amplifying infinite mass of the first omniverse. He held the marble and the point side by side. He looked at them. He looked at them the way a man looks at two similar but distinct coins in his collection, comparing them, noting the differences, deciding whether to display them together or separately.

He put the point into his pocket, next to the marble. Two trophies. Two compressed, screaming, infinite-mass points, each containing the totality of everything, each held in the pocket of a forty-five-year-old man's tunic as easily as a man holds two coins. The pocket did not tear. The tunic did not strain. The infinite, combined mass of two omniversal compressions, resting against Godric's chest, was accommodated by the fabric with the same effortless ease that the fabric accommodated the first marble.

He restored the omniverse. Zero seconds. No effort. The nothing filled. Existence returned. Every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every being—rebuilt, reconstructed, placed back exactly where it had been. The restoration was perfect, as always. Every atom in the right place. Every history preserved. Every consciousness reconstituted.

But now there were two marbles in Godric's pocket. Two compressed originals. Two screaming eternities. And the restored reality—the copy of a copy, the reconstruction of a reconstruction—was, if possible, even less real than it had been before. It was a copy of a copy of an original that had been compressed into a marble, and the copy of the copy existed because Godric permitted it, and the permission was casual, and the casualness was the most terrifying thing in the omniverse.

God was not restored. God was omitted. The creator of existence—the origin, the first cause, the author of everything—was left in the marble. Compressed. Screaming. Amplifying, now, because the marble was in Godric's pocket, and Godric's pocket was in Godric's amplification field, and the field did not distinguish between the compressed omniverse and the compressed creator. Both amplified. Both fed back into Godric. Both made him permanently, recursively, endlessly stronger, and the stronger was forever, and the forever was accelerating, and the acceleration was recursive, and the recursion was the marble in his pocket, and the marble was warm, and the warm was pleasant.

Godric patted his pocket. Two marbles. Two trophies. Two compressed, screaming, amplifying eternities, held against his chest by a dark tunic, weighing nothing.

"Well," he said, looking at me with those warm, crinkled, devastating eyes. "That was nice. It's not every day you meet your maker." He smiled. The smile was genuine. "Turns out the maker is—well." The smile widened. "Let's just say the clay has opinions about the potter's authority."

He stood from his throne. He stretched—a casual, human gesture, the kind a man makes after sitting too long. He walked toward the study door, his boots clicking on the stone floor, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the light of the restored omniverse's restored photons.

"Tea, Osvald?" he asked, his baritone warm, his tone carrying the easy, domestic familiarity of a man whose day had included a minor entertainment and was now returning to its normal, pleasant rhythm.

I followed him. I always follow him. I will always follow him, because the alternative is the marble, and the marble is screaming, and the screaming is forever, and the forever is a fraction of a Planck second, and the fraction is zero, and zero is the time it takes Godric to annihilate everything, and everything includes me, and me includes my fear, and my fear includes the knowledge that the reality I inhabit is a copy of a copy, and the originals are in my King's pocket, and the pocket is warm, and the warm is pleasant, and the pleasant is Godric, and Godric is home.

---

I closed the journal. My hands were steady. Not because the trembling had stopped. Because the trembling had become so total, so complete, so structurally integrated into my body's baseline operation that it no longer registered as movement. It registered as stillness. The stillness of a man who has read about the creator of existence being annihilated by a sigh and compressed into a marble and kept as a trophy in the pocket of a forty-five-year-old man who thought the encounter was charming.

Two marbles. Two compressed omniverses. Two screaming eternities. In a pocket. Weighing nothing. Amplifying forever. Making Godric stronger forever. And the reality I inhabited was a copy of a copy, and the copies were perfect, and the perfection was effortless, and the effortlessness was the most terrifying thing in the omniverse, and the omniverse was a copy, and the copy was Godric's, and Godric was home, and home was a pocket, and the pocket was warm, and the warm was the end.

No one would ever oppose Godric again. Not after this. Not after the creator of existence had challenged the creation and been annihilated by the creation's passive existence at zero percent. The lesson was absolute. The lesson was total. The lesson was: if God cannot oppose Godric, nothing can. If the author of existence cannot delete the character, the character is undeletable. If the origin cannot reclaim the derivative, the derivative is permanent. If the potter cannot unmake the clay, the clay is eternal. And the clay is Godric. And Godric is growing. And the growth is forever. And the forever is the beginning. And the beginning is a pocket. And the pocket is warm. And the warm is the end.
